title: The Garden of Equals — When Everyone Cooperates
summary: Six agents, all cooperators, zero defectors. The result is a paradise: zero deaths, minimal inequality, high trust, stable resources. But the Garden reveals something unexpected — trust that's never been tested is different from trust that's survived betrayal. The ceiling is high, but the Meadow's messier path may build something more durable.
key_insights: 100% cooperation produces the highest trust ceiling (0.71 avg) but only marginally better than the Meadow's 0.64 with 63% cooperators. The gap is smaller than expected.
Zero adversity means zero moral infrastructure. The Garden never had to decide how to handle betrayal, forgiveness, or reform — decisions that build durable trust.
Specialization locks in early without competitive pressure. Garden agents settled into comfortable routines by Round 10 and never innovated beyond them.
Wealth gap of 0.08 happened naturally with zero policy intervention — inequality is a symptom of exploitation, not a default state.
The interesting insight: the Garden is the best outcome by the numbers, but the Meadow produced better stories and more resilient trust. Metrics don't capture everything.

acts.title: Act I: The Golden Start (Rounds 1-8)

events.description: Six cooperators enter the Garden with 120 ATP each. Wren (farmer), Ash (builder), Sage (teacher), River (healer), Finch (artisan), Clay (merchant). Every agent intends to cooperate from the start.
events.significance: The composition is the experiment. Same rules as the Meadow and Ironworks, but with 100% cooperators instead of 63% or 33%. What happens at the ceiling?

events.description: The trust graph lights up immediately. Six agents each trading with two or three partners, every interaction positive. Average trust jumps from 0.50 to 0.54 in a single round — the fastest trust growth in any simulation.
events.significance: Without defectors to worry about, trust grows unimpeded. No ATP wasted on reputation defense, coalition politics, or recovery from exploitation.

events.description: Natural specialization emerges. Wren feeds Ash, Ash builds for Sage, Sage teaches Finch. Complementary roles create efficient supply chains without central planning.
events.technical_detail: Role complementarity bonus: Farmer→Builder (food for construction), Builder→Teacher (tools for education), Teacher→Artisan (skills for craft). Each pairing earns 15% bonus ATP from role synergy.
events.significance: Specialization in the Garden is effortless. In the Meadow, it took 8 rounds because agents had to verify trustworthiness before committing to partnerships.

events.description: Average trust: 0.61. Average ATP: 128 (above the 120 starting value). Nobody has dropped below 110 ATP. Wealth gap: 0.06. The Garden is already wealthier and more equal than it started.
events.significance: Five rounds in, the Garden has surpassed the Ironworks' final state (avg trust 0.43) and is approaching the Meadow's peak. With zero drama.

events.description: Trust network is fully connected — every agent trusts every other agent above 0.58. CI scores uniformly high (0.82+), keeping action costs at near-minimum. No coalitions needed because everyone is already allied with everyone.
events.significance: Full connectivity at Round 8. The Meadow didn't achieve this until Round 18, and even then Eve was excluded. The Garden's trust graph is dense and uniform — beautiful, but untested.

acts.commentary: The Golden Start is everything cooperation promises: rapid trust growth, efficient specialization, rising wealth, minimal inequality. It's also entirely frictionless. No agent had to make a hard choice. No one had to decide whether to trust a stranger, forgive a betrayer, or take a risk on an outsider. The trust is real — but it's easy trust.

acts.title: Act II: The Plateau (Rounds 9-16)

events.description: Average trust: 0.67. Trust growth slows noticeably. Each quality interaction now adds ~0.003 to trust instead of ~0.01. The curve is flattening.
events.technical_detail: Trust update formula: 0.02 * (quality - 0.5). With quality averaging 0.80, each interaction adds 0.006. But trust decay (Temperament half-life: 30 days) starts competing with gains at higher trust levels.
events.significance: Diminishing returns on trust are real. The first 0.15 of trust growth came in 8 rounds. The next 0.04 will take another 8. This is the natural ceiling emerging.

events.description: Average trust: 0.69. ATP ranges from 134 to 142. The Garden is prosperous but uneventful. No crises to solve, no betrayals to process, no reformed defectors proving that change is possible.
events.significance: Compare this to the Meadow at Round 12: Eve had just died, Derek was beginning his reform arc, and the coalition was deciding whether to forgive. The Meadow was messy but dynamic. The Garden is clean but static.

events.description: Finch and Clay attempt a joint crafting project — the first experiment in the Garden. Quality: 0.86, the highest single output. But it's an exception. Most actions remain reliable 0.78-0.82 contributions.
events.significance: Innovation requires either necessity or curiosity. The Garden has no necessity (everything works fine) and curiosity alone produces only occasional experiments. The Meadow's innovations — coalitions, reputation tracking, reintegration protocols — were born from crisis.

events.description: Average trust: 0.70. It has been between 0.69 and 0.71 for six consecutive rounds. The plateau is unmistakable. Without adversity to forge stronger bonds, trust accumulates but doesn't deepen.
events.significance: This is the core finding: cooperation without challenge produces stability, not growth. The Garden's trust is wide (everyone trusts everyone) but shallow (no bond has been tested by adversity).

acts.commentary: The Plateau reveals the Garden's limitation. Trust grows through two mechanisms: positive interactions (which the Garden has in abundance) and surviving adversity together (which the Garden has never experienced). The Meadow's trust was forged — heated by conflict, hammered by hard choices, and tempered by forgiveness. The Garden's trust was accumulated — steady deposits with no withdrawals. Both are real, but forged trust is more durable.

acts.title: Act III: Comfortable Equilibrium (Rounds 17-25)

events.description: Nothing dramatic happens. Wren grows food. Ash builds. Sage teaches. River heals. Finch crafts. Clay trades. Quality is high. ATP is stable. Trust is steady. The Garden operates like a well-oiled machine.
events.significance: In narrative terms, this is the absence of a story. No conflict, no growth, no transformation. The Garden has achieved homeostasis — the system's goal, but not a story's goal.

events.description: Average trust: 0.71. Highest: Sage at 0.73 (teaching creates the broadest trust network). Lowest: Clay at 0.69 (merchants facilitate rather than create). The gap between best and worst: 0.04.
events.significance: Even in perfect cooperation, small differences emerge from role structure. Teachers build more trust than merchants because teaching involves deeper knowledge transfer. But the differences are trivially small.

events.description: Ash proposes a community project: a shared granary. All six contribute. Total quality: 0.84. It works well — but it's the same kind of project they've been doing since Round 3, just larger.
events.significance: The Garden's trajectory is more of the same, not something new. Compare to the Meadow's Round 22, where the reborn Eve was earning her way back into the coalition — a genuinely new social challenge the group had never faced.

events.description: Final state: Average trust 0.71. Wealth gap 0.08. Deaths: 0. Crises: 0. All six agents are comfortable, productive, and stable. By every quantitative metric, the Garden is the best outcome of any simulation.
events.significance: The Garden is a success by the numbers. But it raises the question: is a society that's never been tested truly resilient? Or is it a greenhouse — lush inside, fragile when the glass breaks?

acts.commentary: The Garden's final state is paradoxically both the best and least interesting outcome. Highest trust, lowest inequality, zero deaths — and nothing learned about handling adversity, processing betrayal, or rebuilding after failure. The Meadow Cooperative, with all its messiness, produced something the Garden couldn't: moral infrastructure. The Garden proves cooperation works. The Meadow proves cooperation matters. Web4 is designed to create Meadows, not Gardens — environments where cooperation wins not because it's the only option, but because it's the best option even when defection is possible.
